1: Hear this word — shim'u et-haddavar hazeh: the prophetic call to attention that opens each major section (Amos 3:1; Amos 4:1; Amos 5:1). Asher dibber YHWH 'aleykhem (which the LORD has spoken against you) — the preposition 'al (against/upon) signals a covenant-lawsuit or judgment speech rather than a word of blessing. 'Al kol-hamishpachah (against the whole family) — the covenant family (mishpachah) is accountable as a unit; the exodus defines the boundaries of responsibility.
2: You alone have I known — raq etkhem yadati mikol mishpachot ha'adamah: the pivotal verse of the chapter and the theological heart of the book. Yada' (know) in its covenant sense — relational election, not merely cognitive awareness (cf. Genesis 18:19; Jeremiah 1:5). The raq (only, alone) is emphatic: Israel's election was exclusive among all families of earth. 'Al-ken ephqod 'aleykhem et kol-avonoteykhem (therefore I will hold you accountable for all your iniquities) — election intensifies accountability, not reduces it. The privilege of covenant relationship creates heightened obligation, not immunity.
3: Can two people walk together — hayo-lekhun shne'im yachdav bilti im-noa'du: the series of rhetorical questions (vv3-6) is Amos' argumentative method — from self-evident natural observations to the conclusion that YHWH's word creates prophetic compulsion. Noa'du (unless they have made an appointment/agreed) — the verb ya'ad (to set a time, appoint, agree to meet); the metaphor is two people with agreed direction. The question proves: effects have causes; YHWH's word has produced the prophet.
4: Does a lion roar when it has no prey — heyish'ag arye ba-ya'ar ve-tereph eyn lo: lion behavior as analogy for prophetic compulsion. The roar announces the kill; silence means no prey. Yitten kefir qolo mimme'onato bilti im-lakhad (does the young lion growl from its lair without having caught anything?) — kefir (young lion) vs. arye (mature lion) — the pair covers both stages. The roar of judgment (3:8 conclusion) is grounded in the reality of the prey already taken.
5: Does a bird fall to the ground without a trap — hettippol tsippor 'al-pach ha'arets: trap-and-bird imagery continuing the cause-effect series. Ve-moqesh eyn lah (when no trap has been set) — moqesh (snare, trap) is also used metaphorically for seductions/idols. Ha-ya'aleh-pach min-ha'adamah velakod lo yilkod (does a trap spring from the ground without catching anything?) — the trap springs because something triggered it; nothing happens without cause.
6: When a ram's horn sounds in a city — im-yitqa' shofar be'ir: the shofar alarm (military attack warning) produces fear by reflex. Ve-'am lo yecherod (do the people not tremble/fear?) — the expected answer: yes, they do. The final question is the theological climax: im-tihyeh ra'ah be'ir va-YHWH lo 'asah (if disaster strikes a city, has the LORD not done it?) — ra'ah (disaster, evil, calamity) — not moral evil but calamity/judgment. YHWH's sovereign governance of history: even disaster has divine agency behind it (cf. Isaiah 45:7).
7: The Lord GOD does nothing without disclosing his plans — ki lo ya'aseh Adonai YHWH dabar ki im-galah sodo el-'avadav haNevi'im: the climactic principle of the riddle-series. Sod (secret counsel, council) — the divine council concept (Jeremiah 23:18, 22; Psalms 25:14); YHWH's sod is his deliberative council-decision, first disclosed to the prophets before execution. The prophets are admitted to the divine inner circle (sod) and tasked with announcing what they heard. This verse legitimates the entire prophetic enterprise: Amos speaks because YHWH disclosed.
8: The lion has roared — who will not be afraid? — arye sha'ag mi lo yira': the answer to all the rhetorical questions of vv3-7 is stated: divine speech produces prophetic compulsion. Adonai YHWH dibber mi lo yinnabe' (the Lord GOD has spoken — who can withhold prophecy?) — nibba' (to prophesy, speak as a prophet) — the Niphal/Hithpael of naba'; prophetic speech is the natural response to divine speech, as fear is the natural response to the lion's roar. Amos' defense of his prophetic calling is built on this principle.
9: Proclaim to the citadels of Ashdod and Egypt — Amos summons foreign powers as witnesses to Israel's shame. Asafim al-hare Shomron (gather on the mountains of Samaria) — the witnesses are to observe from the heights. Mehumot rabbot (great disorders/tumults) and ashuqim (oppressions) — mehumah (chaos, confusion) is the term for covenant-curse disorder (Deuteronomy 28:20); 'oshek (oppression of the vulnerable) is the corresponding term for economic injustice. Even Philistine Ashdod and Egypt would be shocked by what they see.
10: They do not know how to act with integrity — lo-yad'u 'asot-nekhochah: nekhochah (what is right, straight, correct) — the term for straight, upright dealing; they do not even know what integrity is. Ne'um-YHWH (declares the LORD) — the prophetic utterance formula; the accusation carries divine authority. Ha'otzrim chamas va-shod be-armnoteihem (those who hoard violence and plunder in their fortresses) — chamas (violence, unjust gain) and shod (plunder, devastation) stored in the very fortresses built with stolen wealth.
11: An enemy will surround the land — tsar usseviv ha'arets: the judgment mirrors the crime. They hoarded plunder in their fortresses; those same fortresses will be plundered. Ve-horid mimekh uzzakh (he will strip away your strength/defense from you) — uzzah (strength, might) stripped away; the strength used to oppress becomes the first casualty. Ve-nivazzu armonotaykh (your fortresses will be plundered) — the ironic reversal: the storage site of shod (plunder) becomes the object of plundering.
12: As the shepherd snatches two legs or a scrap of an ear — ka'asher yatsel haro'eh mippi ha'arye shtey kraa'im o vadal-ozen: a vivid analogy. Mosaic law required shepherds to produce the remains of sheep killed by predators to prove they had not stolen the animal (Exodus 22:13). The two legs and ear-scrap are not rescue but proof of death — the animal is entirely gone. Ken yinnatselv bene Yisra'el (so will the Israelites be rescued) — the irony: this is what passes for 'rescue'; survival means a tattered remnant, not genuine deliverance. Yosevei Shomron bipheat mittah uviqdat eres — the elite of Samaria, reclining on their expensive imported furniture, will be what the shepherd shows as 'evidence.'
13: Hear and testify against the house of Jacob — shim'u ve-ha'idu bebet Ya'aqov: the court language continues. Ne'um-Adonai YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot — the full divine title: YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot (LORD God of armies/hosts) appears first here in Amos; the military title frames the judgment that follows. The summons is to testify, not just to hear.
14: On the day I call Israel to account — beyom paqdi pish'ei Yisra'el 'alav: paqad (visit, reckon, hold accountable) — the term for divine intervention in judgment or blessing. Ve-paqad'ti 'al mizbechot Bet-El (I will strike down the altars of Bethel) — the apostate shrine of Bethel is targeted specifically; where Israel sought YHWH through the golden calf will be demolished. Ve-niggede'u qarnot hamizbeach (the altar's horns will be cut off and fall to the ground) — the horns of the altar were where sacrificial blood was applied and where a person could flee for asylum (1 Kings 1:50-51; Amos 2:28); both sanctuary and sacrifice are terminated.
15: The winter house and the summer house — beit ha-choref (winter house) and beit ha-qayits (summer house) — the royal and wealthy elite maintained seasonal residences; the existence of both signals extreme luxury. Batei hashschen (houses adorned with ivory) — sheshen (ivory/tooth) used as furniture inlay and wall decoration; this is confirmed archaeologically at Samaria where ivory plaques were found. Ve-safu batim rabbim (and the great mansions will come to an end) — suf (come to an end, be swept away); the accumulation of property that secured status will be swept away in a single judgment.
